coredata.dc:title: Development of Ag nanoparticle-carbon quantum dot nanocomplex as fluorescence sensor for determination of gemcitabine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Shekarbeygi, Zahra
Karami, Changiz
Esmaeili, Esmaeil
Moradi, Sajad
Shahlaei, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description: Gemcitabine hydrochloride is an established chemotherapeutic agent in several solid tumors. In spite of outstanding therapeutic efficacy, there are some serious fetal side effects with gemcitabine in higher concentrations which necessitate developing a sensitive sensor for its quantification. Herein, a fluorescent metal-nanoparticles conjugated carbon quantum dot (MN-CQD) was prepared by a mixture of citric acid/ammonia sulfate and different metals using hydrothermal method. Based on the primary experiments, the efficiency of Ag nanoparticle-CQDs for gemcitabine determination was found to be much better than others. The AgNp-CQDs fluorescence was quenched by gemcitabine anticancer drug via photo-induced charge transfer which renders the system into fluorescence “OFF” status. Under the experimental conditions, the linear range of detection was 0.003–0.1 μM in an aqueous solution with a correlation coefficient of 0.96 and a limit of detection equal to 0.002 µM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for gemcitabine determination was 3.4% (n = 3). Finally, after optimizing the conditions, the concentration of analyte was determined in real samples including human plasma and urine. These results confirm that the as prepared fluorescence based nanosensor can be used for sensitive quantification of gemcitabine in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nano-probe
High-fluorescence composite
Gemcitabine
Fluorescence quenching

Assessment of the quenching GEM mechanism
Comparing the CDs in GEM detection
Quenching mechanism of Ag-CQD by GEM

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence properties of carbon dots at different excitation wavelengths (λex): a) Cu- CQDs, b) Ag-CQDs, c) Mn- CQDs, d) Ni-CQDs.
Excitation (left line) and emission (right line) spectra of synthesized metal conjugated carbon quantum dots a) Cu- CQDs, b) Ag-CQDs, c) Mn- CQDs, d) Ni-CQDs.
a) UV spectra of different carbon quantum dots; b) the photo stability of different dots against light.
Changes in emission values of different carbon dots in the presence of gemcitabine (a) and emission spectrum of Ag-CQDs in the presence of drug (b).
Physicochemical characterization of Ag-CQDs; a) TEM imaging, b) diagram of the particle size distribution, (c) the FTIR spectrum and d) XRD pattern of Ag-CQDs.

Optimization of test conditions a) Adjustment of pH and b) incubation time, c) the fluorescence emission diagrams of Ag-CQDs in the presence of different concentrations of GEM and d) calibration curve of GEM determination by Ag-CQDs.

a) Stern–Volmer curves for Ag-CQDs in the presence of incremental concentrations of gemcitabine at three different temperatures, b) the plot of
log
F 0
- F
F
versus log [GEM] and c) the Van-Hoff diagram.
Schematic illustration of the detection process for gemcitabine hydrochloride.
Thermodynamic parameters of GEM – Ag-CQDs system.
Determination of GEM in real samples.
List of different methods for determine GEM.

head.ce:title: Development of Ag nanoparticle-carbon quantum dot nanocomplex as fluorescence sensor for determination of gemcitabine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: professor of medicinal chemistry, Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran.
Corresponding author at: Assistant professor of Nanobiotechnology, Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gemcitabine hydrochloride is an established chemotherapeutic agent in several solid tumors. In spite of outstanding therapeutic efficacy, there are some serious fetal side effects with gemcitabine in higher concentrations which necessitate developing a sensitive sensor for its quantification. Herein, a fluorescent metal-nanoparticles conjugated carbon quantum dot (MN-CQD) was prepared by a mixture of citric acid/ammonia sulfate and different metals using hydrothermal method. Based on the primary experiments, the efficiency of Ag nanoparticle-CQDs for gemcitabine determination was found to be much better than others. The AgNp-CQDs fluorescence was quenched by gemcitabine anticancer drug via photo-induced charge transfer which renders the system into fluorescence “OFF” status. Under the experimental conditions, the linear range of detection was 0.003–0.1 μM in an aqueous solution with a correlation coefficient of 0.96 and a limit of detection equal to 0.002 µM. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for gemcitabine determination was 3.4% (n = 3). Finally, after optimizing the conditions, the concentration of analyte was determined in real samples including human plasma and urine. These results confirm that the as prepared fluorescence based nanosensor can be used for sensitive quantification of gemcitabine in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Gemcitabine hydrochloride (2′-deoxy-2′,2′-difluoro cytidine hydrochloride) (GEM) is a widely used anticancer drug for treating the various malignancies including ovarian, lung, pancreatic, urothelial and breast cancer [1]. On the other hand, because of sever damages to some normal cells such as bone marrow, digestive tract and hair follicles, controlling the concentration of gemcitabine in patients serum is crucial [2]. In recent years, several methods including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3–5], mass spectroscopy (MS) [6], Liquid chromatography (LC) [7,8] and electro analytical approaches [9] have been studied for GEM detection. The aforesaid methods are associated with difficulties such as expensive instrumentation, time-consuming and low sensitivity. Hence it is necessary to develop further sensitive, time and cost effective and simple spectroscopic methods for rapid detection of this medication (see Scheme 1 ).

One of the well-known specific and sensitive spectroscopic approaches in the field of analytical chemistry and medicine is the fluorescence spectroscopy [10]. There are many other advantages related to this method including need to the small amount of sample, speed in analysis, and no complicated sample preparation [11]. Fluorescent nanoparticles have received much attention in recent two decades for using in analytical chemistry and biology [12].

In recent years, semiconductor nanocrystals known as quantum dots (QDs) have been extensively studied due to their unique optical and electrical properties. As an excellent alternatives, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are other fluorescent nanomaterials which used in different fields of science [13,14]. In addition to their biosafety and easiness of preparation, the CQDs have received a lot of attention in recent studies because they can be modified via simple deposition or coating methods by metal particles [15,16] or organic molecules [15]. Carbon quantum dots also benefits from their high quantum yield, low cost, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, and good photostability [17].

Surface modification is the popular method for adjusting the optical and biological properties of carbon dots as these properties are highly depend on surface chemistry and interaction of CQDs with other micro and macromolecules [18]. The surface of most carbon dots is rich of oxygen-containing functional groups that allow covalent bonding with other groups [19]. Covalent bonding of amino groups to the surface is one of the most common approaches to improve the luminosity properties of carbon dots [20]. In general, the optical performance of CDs can be controlled by making changes to the CD surface or their conjugation with other metallic nano particles [21]. Recent researches have shown that metals can couple with nearby photons and enhance the fluorescent intensity of CQDs through electromagnetic field enhanced excitation or energy transfer [21]. This may provide a useful output to solve the problems related to low fluorescence intensity of some biocompatible CQDs [22]. Various researches are conducted to investigate the effects of different metals on physicochemical properties of fluorescent nano materials [23]. For example Ni-doped quantum dots were used for optical determination of gemcitabine [24]. Others such as Ag [25] and Cu [26] were used for enhancing of quantum yield of different CQDs and detection of biological molecules.

In this research, Carbon quantum dots associated to metal nanoparticles (M-CQDs) were prepared with a mixture of citric acid and ammonia sulfate in equal ratios and different metals by hydrothermal method. In the next step, the efficiency of M-CQDs for GEM identification has been studied. Finally, after optimization of the experimental condition for detection of GEM, the validations were performed in real human plasma and urine samples.

Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), dipotassium hydrogen phosphate trihydrate (K2HPO4 3H2O), citric acid and ammonium sulfate were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Manganese chloride, nickel sulfate, copper sulfate, silver nitrate and Gemcitabine were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the range of 300–600 nm with excitation wavelength at 320 nm by an LS55 spectrometer (PerkinElmer, US). The Transmission electron microscope (TEM) (LEO912-AB) was used for precise measurement of particle size and morphology of M-CQDs. UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained with Agilent 8453 (Germany) spectrophotometer. The Shimadzu IR instrument was used for recording of FT-IR spectra in the wavenumber range 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were collected with a Rigaku Ultima IV XRD diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) equipped with graphite monochromatized high-intensity Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5417 Å).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrothermal synthesis and characterization of M-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Four types of metal conjugated fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal using citric acid as carbon source. Briefly, at first 20 ml of 0.1 M citric acid solution and 20 ml of 0.1 M ammonium sulfate were mixed and stirred for 10 min. Then, 10 ml of each metal solution (0.025 mM) were added to the prepared mixture, poured into a 50 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and incubated for 2 h at 200 °C. The autoclave was then cooled to ambient temperature and the product was filtered using a cylindrical filtration membrane filter (0.22 μm). Finally in order to remove non reacted precursors, all synthesized CDs were dialyzed against distilled water at room temperature for 24 h. The resultant was then freeze dried and maintain in 4 °C for further uses.

After preparation of M-CQDs (20 µgmL−1), the optical properties of CQDs were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy techniques. The quantum yield (QY %) of obtained CQDs were calculated using the integrated emission intensity of quinine sulfate as standard. To investigate the photo stability of the prepared M-CQDs, their fluorescence intensities were checked at different times under visible light.

The effects of GEM on the fluorescence intensity of M-CQDs were investigated by adding of the drug (0.1 μM) to the M-CQDs (20 µgmL−1) solutions. After 5 min, the fluorescence intensities were measured at different excitation/emission wavelengths. Also the experimental conditions of pH, temperature and incubation time for the determintion of GEM were adjusted.

The fluorescence of Ag-CQDs (20 μgml−1, pH = 7.4) was recorded each time, after addition of GEM solution (1 μL × 1 mM). According to the changes in the flourscence spectra of Ag-CQDs, the calibration curve of GEM was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the quenching GEM mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the fluorescence quenching mechanism (Dynamic or Static), the thermodynamic parameters of interaction between Ag-CQDs and GEM were measured in three different temperatures (290, 300 and 310k).

For determination of the GEM in plasma, first a healthy human blood plasma sample was prepared from the Blood Transfusion Organization of Iran and stored at −20 °C. One ml of plasma sample and 800 μL of acetonitrile were mixed and centrifuged for 5 min in 5000 rpm after its volume was reached to 15 ml by PBS buffer. Different amounts of 6, 16.2, 36, 72 and 108 μL of GEM (1 mM) were then added to 3 ml of the as prepared serum and incubated for 5 min in room temperature. Finally, 5 μL of each sample was added to the Ag-CQDs (20 µgmL−1) each time, and the spectra were recorded. For determination of GEM in urine, 2 ml of a fresh urine sample (healthy human) was filtered and then treated similar to that of plasma samples.

In this study, several metal nanoparticles associated carbon quantum dots including; Copper, Silver, Manganese and Nickel were synthesized and their optical properties were studied. The best of as prepared fluorescent carbon dots in the term of optical properties and response to gemcitabine were then optimized for quantitative determination of the drug. Fig. 1 shows the fluorescence spectra of the M-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths. From this figure the Ag-CQD has higher fluorescence intensity and a sharper peak than the other compounds. In addition, the excitation/emission spectra of the M-CQDs are shown in Fig. 2 . It was observed that all the aqueous M-CQD solutions show their excitation and emission peaks around 330 and 430 nm respectively. Further optical characteristics of Mn-CQDs, Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs and Ni-CQDs were explored using UV–visible spectroscopy. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of the Ag-CQDs at 340 nm and for Mn-CQDs, Cu-CQDs and Ni-CQDs at 335, 330 and 325 nm, respectively. The UV–vis peaks for M-CQDs can be related to the π → π* transition of C=C bonds and n → π* transition of C = O bonds.

The ratio of the number of photons emitted to photons absorbed is defined as quantum yield and follows the below equation:
(1)
Φ
C D s
=
Φ S
×
F
C D s
F S
×
A S
A
C D s

In this equation, FCDs and ACDs are the fluorescence and absorbance intensity of M-CQDs, respectively [27]. The quinine sulfate was considered as standard with a quantum yield (ΦS) of 55%. The calculated quantum yield for Cu-CQDs, Ag-CQDs, Mn-CQDs, and Ni-CQDs were respectively 35, 46, 41 and 37%. The presence of the free electrons in the metals might cause the increase in quantum yield of the M-CQDs. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3b, the photostability of the as prepared CQDs is excellent as their intensities maintain almost constant after 12 days under the light at room temperature. As can be seen, the results indicate that the Ag-CQD has better photostability than others.

After evaluating the optical properties of the synthesized M-CQDs, their ability for detecting the GEM was investigated. Among the tested nano M-CQDs only the Ag-CQD showed a good response to the drug (Fig. 4 ). Therefore, the Ag-CQD was chosen for further experiments and characterizations with TEM, FTIR, DLS and XRD.

Two points can be deduced from the TEM image shown in Fig. 5 a; the particle diameter is about 6–9 nm and the particle shape is possessed spherical morphologies. The results of dynamic light scattering (DLS) indicated that the hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs are in the range of 50 nm. Also the calculated zeta potential of Ag-CQDs was −16 ± 5 mV indicating a great electrostatic repulsion between particles that results in their good dispersion and colloidal stability in aqueous solutions. The FTIR was used to verify the functional groups on the surface of the Ag-CQDs structure. According to Fig. 5c, the index peaks of CQDs are located at 1667 cm−1 correspond to –C=O, the peak at 1498 cm−1 and 1454 cm−1 corresponds to –C=C, and the peak at 1248 cm−1 is relate to C–O. There is however another important peak in position 600 cm−1 which is related to Ag-R bonding and confirms conjugation of CQDs with Ag nano particles. The XRD pattern of Ag-CQDs (Fig. 5d), displays the diffraction features at 2θ = 24.1, 26.5, 32.8, 38.7, 44.3, 52.6 respectively. Based on the literatures the graphitic peaks related to carbonic sheets in carbon dots appear in the 2θ around 26° [28]. About the reduced silver nano crystals (Ago) the more distinct characteristics peaks of (111), (200) planes are observed in 38.7 and 44.3 respectively (JCPDS, No. 04-0783). Other peaks in 2θ = 32.8 and 52.6 are related to non-reduced AgNO3 [29,30]. From these results it can be concluded that the final structure is a complex mixture of the reduced Ago nano particles and nano CDs. Based on the Bragg’s equation (nλ = 2d sin θ) the shifting of peak positions towards the smaller 2θ is a sign of increasing in distances between the planes. Considering this equation, from the results obtained here it can be concluded that there are some metal ions intercalated into graphitic sheets of CDs and causes some peak relocation from 2θ = 26 to 24 in some sheets. Also from the figure, as can be seen that there are some broadening in crystallographic peaks related to all materials in nano Ag-CQD. This is because of the presence of strains on the crystal lattices which confirms the tight conjugations between AgNP and CQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the CDs in GEM detection
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the quenching effects of GEM on Mn-CQDs, Ag-CQDs, Cu-CQDs and, Ni-CQDs have first been studied and then the best “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for GEM detection was selected for further experiments (Fig. 4). From the Fig. 4 and by comparing the quenching effect of the GEM on different CDs, it can be seen that the carbon quantum dots containing silver has a better and more noticeable response to the GEM.

The GEM effect on Ag-CQDs was measured at different pH and incubation times to achieve optimal conditions for best determination efficacy and the results are shown in Fig. 6 a and 6b. The results show that the optimum values for pH is 6 in an incubation time of 7 min. After obtaining the optimal conditions for measuring the GEM using Ag-CQDs, the “turn-off” fluorescent sensor was assessed to obtaining its linear range of drug detection. The results in Fig. 6c and 6d confirm a linear correlation in the range of 0.003 µM to 0.1 µM of GEM. The calibration equation equals to Δ
F = 18.13
G e m
+ 97.822
(R2 = 0.96) and also the LOD (according to S/N = 3) and RSD% are 0.002 (µM) and 3.45%, respectively. The results demonstrated that the proposed sensor was capable of detecting GEM in nano molar concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanism of Ag-CQD by GEM
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence emission quenching is divided into two categories, dynamic and static. In dynamic quenching, an increase in temperature causes faster molecular diffusion in medium and increase in the collisions between fluorophore and quencher. This leads to increasing the quenching constant at higher temperatures. Unlike, in the static state, an increase in temperature weakens the stability of molecular complexes and as a result, quenching rate is reduced. For this purpose, the Stern-Volmer equation was used:
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
G E M
where the F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of free and GEM treated Ag-CQDs, respectively. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [GEM] is the concentration of GEM. In order to find out the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the experiments were performed at 290, 300, and 310 K, and the results are shown in Fig. 7 a. The linearity in the F0 / F plot against [GEM] confirms the one-to-one interaction between GEM and Ag-CQDs. The calculated values of the Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv ) at 3 temperatures are shown in Table1 . From the data in the table, it can be seen that in the presence of GEM, the values of Ksv increased with increasing temperature. This indicates that the fluorescence of Ag-CQDs in the presence of GEM is quenched by a collisional dynamic manner [31].

The binding constant values (Kbinding) and the average number of GEM binding at different temperatures were calculated using the following equation:
(3)
log
F 0
- F
F
= log
K b
+ n log
[ G E M ]

The results are shown in Fig. 7b and Table 1. As in Table 1, the binding constant and the number of binding sites (n) decreased with increasing the temperature and this is a sign for a decrease in the stability of GEM-Ag-CODs complex [32].

Non-covalent interactions involved in the intermolecular collisions mainly originate from hydrogen bonding, Van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions, and hydrophobic interactions. In this regard, the binding constant for GEM and Ag-CQDs complex formation was measured at 3 different temperatures and the thermodynamic parameters (ΔH° and ΔS°) related to the binding process were obtained through Van Hoff and Gibbs equations (equations (4) and (5)) [33] .
(4)
Ln
K b
= -
Δ H
0
RT
+
Δ S
0
R
(5)
Δ G
0
=
Δ H
0
- T
Δ S
0

Negative value of ΔG ° means the inherent spontaneity of the GEM binding process to Ag-CQDs. When ΔH° ≤ 0 and ΔS° > 0, the electrostatic force is dominant in the interaction, if ΔH° < 0 and ΔS° < 0 then Van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonds are predominant, and when ΔH° > 0 and ΔS° > 0 the Hydrophobic interactions are major forces in the bonding process. Accordingly as both obtained values for ΔS ° and ΔH ° are negative, it can be concluded that the week Van der Waals interactions and hydrogen bonding are the main forces involved in binding of GEM to Ag-CQDs (Table1).

GEM can act as a cationic electron acceptor, thus the collisional interactions of Ag-CQDs with GEM induces ultrafast photo-induced electron transfer from Ag-CQDs to the electron acceptor. This can prevents the normal recombination of the electron-hole in carbon dots and results in a quenching of the FL intensity. As the energy levels for such phenomenon is specific for each fluorophore and its quenchers, the as prepare CQDs were quenched by GEM specifically.

To validate the usability of Ag-CQD sensor in complicated biological samples, the developed method was applied for determination of GEM in human plasma and urine samples. As stated above, a certain amount of the GEM by standard addition method was added to the human plasma and resulted in a change in the fluorescence intensity. As can be seen in Table 2 , the recoveries of GEM in the human plasma and urine samples was in the range of 90 to 110% and their RSDs ranged between 2.36 and 4.17%. This indicated that the matrix did not interfere with the detection of various concentrations of GEM. This result demonstrates that the proposed method can be applied to the rapid monitoring of GEM as anticancer drug in biological samples.

In this study different metal nanoparticles-conjugated carbon dots were evaluated and compared against their ability for the quantitative determination of gemcitabine. In summary, the determination of gemcitabine as an anticancer drug by Ag-nanoparticles associated with carbon dots showed the best response in comparison to others (Table 3 ). The as-prepared CQDs represented excellent detection capability towards the analyte over a wide concentration range of 0.003–0.1 μM. From this table, it can be seen that the proposed method has a higher sensitivity and lower detection limit than others especially those of QD based nanosensor. However because of cost and time effective manner along with lower toxicity, the prepared sensor in this study can be better option for determination of gemcitabine in biological samples. By using the developed method, high sensitivity, broad linear range, and low detection limit of gemcitabine is achieved in human plasma and urine samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no any competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the Research Council of Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran, for the financial support. Grant number [990847].
